Title: AI-Driven Inventory Optimization in Supply Chains
Source title: 2025 International Conference on Business Intelligence for Technology Innovation, ICBITI 2025
Affiliations: Taibah University, College of Science and Computer Engineering, Yanbu,Al-Munawwarahh, Al-Madinah, 42353, Saudi Arabia; Skyline University College, School of Business, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates; Al-Ahliyya Amman University, Faculty of Hospitality and Tourism Management, Amman, Jordan; Applied Science Private University, Faculty of Business, Amman, Jordan
Authors with affiliations: Alqudah M.K., Taibah University, College of Science and Computer Engineering, Yanbu,Al-Munawwarahh, Al-Madinah, 42353, Saudi Arabia; Alzoubi H.M., Skyline University College, School of Business, Sharjah, United Arab Emirates; Alzghoul A.M., Al-Ahliyya Amman University, Faculty of Hospitality and Tourism Management, Amman, Jordan; Samarah M., Applied Science Private University, Faculty of Business, Amman, Jordan
Abstract: The successful flow of goods and services through the supply chain is inextricably linked to efficient inventory management. This is particularly the case in a globalized world with dynamic market demands. Conventional approaches to inventory optimization are often incapable of keeping pace with fast changes in the supply chain variables which is one reason inefficiencies like stockouts, overstocking, and increased costs are prevalent. The paper discusses the role of AI in inventory optimization and assesses its capability to enhance supply chain management. It is through a combination of machine learning, predictive analytics, and real-time data that AI-driven systems can better predict demand, adjust inventory levels, and improve decision-making. The work looks at current AI models in inventory management, evaluating their performance and the challenges faced including data quality, systems' integration and bias. To illustrate the theories in a real-world context, a case study was conducted on AI-driven inventory optimization, giving an insight into its functioning at a retail supply center. Improvements in efficiency, cost-cutting, and customer satisfaction were noted. According to the findings, the use of AI in the development of resilient and agile supply chains is highlighted, and future research directions and practical applications for businesses wishing to remain competitive in a fast-changing marketplace are discussed.  © 2025 IEEE.

Title: The Role of AI and ML in Shaping the Future of the Food Industry
Source title: Current Nutrition and Food Science
Affiliations: School of Computer Science and Engineering (SCOPE), Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Tamil Nadu, Vellore, India
Authors with affiliations: Vijayvargiya S., School of Computer Science and Engineering (SCOPE), Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Tamil Nadu, Vellore, India; Sinha S., School of Computer Science and Engineering (SCOPE), Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Tamil Nadu, Vellore, India; Sivakumar V., School of Computer Science and Engineering (SCOPE), Vellore Institute of Technology (VIT), Tamil Nadu, Vellore, India
Abstract: With the integration of Machine Learning (ML) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) in the food industry, from production to retail, the sector is undergoing a transformative shift. These technologies enhance efficiency through better decision-making and increased automation, helping overcome some of the current challenges in terms of sustainability, food security, and cost reduction. AI and ML are being applied in food processing to optimize production processes, monitor food quality, and detect contaminants, thus achieving higher safety standards and reducing waste. In the supply chain, these technologies enable real-time tracking, predictive analytics for demand forecasting, and optimization of distribution networks to ensure that food reaches consumers in a timely and cost-effective manner. Another area is the use of AI to revolutionize inventory control in retail, helping retailers anticipate consumer demand and prevent food spoilage, thereby maintaining effective stock control. In addition, the current consumer interest in AI-assisted nutrition analysis and personalized recipe recommendations suggests that they are eating healthier. The potential for even greater impacts is vast with the advancement of these technologies. The future directions to be taken forward in this area of concern include precision agriculture, AI for food traceability, and machine learning for predictive models, with further advancements in data science to enhance efficiency, minimize carbon footprints, and maximize security from contamination. The future of food indeed depends significantly on AI and ML; hence, the future is also daunting in its prospects. 2025, Bentham Science Publishers

Authors: Ding J.; Li Z.; Lee E.-S.
Author full names: Ding, Jiangmin (59184698600); Li, Zhaoqi (59237071000); Lee, Eon-Seong (55177924000)
Title: Seeing the Unseen: AI Assimilation and Supply–Demand Visibility for Effective Risk Management in Manufacturing Supply Chains
Source title: Systems
Affiliations: BK21 Digital Finance Education and Research Center, College of Business, Pusan National University, Busan, 46241, South Korea; Department of Business, Gachon University, Seongnam, 13120, South Korea; College of Business, Pusan National University, Busan, 46241, South Korea
Authors with affiliations: Ding J., BK21 Digital Finance Education and Research Center, College of Business, Pusan National University, Busan, 46241, South Korea; Li Z., Department of Business, Gachon University, Seongnam, 13120, South Korea; Lee E.-S., College of Business, Pusan National University, Busan, 46241, South Korea
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has become a strategic resource for enhancing supply chain resilience in environments characterized by growing uncertainty and complexity. Building on the resource-based view (RBV) and organizational information processing theory (OIPT), this study examines how AI assimilation as a firm-level strategic capability improves supply–demand visibility and strengthens supply chain risk management (SCRM). Using survey data collected from 129 manufacturing firms in China, the proposed research framework is tested through structural equation modeling. The results show that AI assimilation significantly enhances both supply–demand visibility and SCRM, with visibility playing a partial mediating role in translating AI-enabled capabilities into more effective risk control. These findings indicate that AI contributes to resilience not merely through technological deployment but through its integration into organizational processes that support information processing and coordination. From a managerial perspective, the study suggests that firms should approach AI as an ongoing strategic capability development process rather than a one-time technological investment. By embedding AI into core supply chain functions such as production planning, inventory management, and demand forecasting, firms can improve visibility, anticipate disruptions, and shift toward more proactive and resilient risk management practices. This study advances the literature by integrating RBV and OIPT to explain the strategic mechanisms through which AI assimilation enhances visibility in SCRM, providing empirical evidence from a manufacturing context. © 2026 by the authors.

Title: Enhancing Operational Efficiency in the Supply Chain: A Systematic Review on Artificial Intelligence Applications; [Mejora de la eficiencia operativa en la cadena de suministro: una revisión sistemática sobre las aplicaciones de la inteligencia artificial]
Source title: Proceedings of the LACCEI international Multi-conference for Engineering, Education and Technology
Affiliations: Universidad Tecnológica del Perú, Peru; Peru
Authors with affiliations: Cochachin-Maguiña M.I., Universidad Tecnológica del Perú, Peru; Lopez-Santacruz J.A., Universidad Tecnológica del Perú, Peru; Coaquira-Torres E., Universidad Tecnológica del Perú, Peru; Gómez-Montoya J.P., Universidad Tecnológica del Perú, Peru; Rojas-Rimbaldo J.M., Peru
Abstract: Currently, supply chains have become increasingly complex due to globalization, which has driven companies to seek innovative alternatives to maintain their competitive advantage. In this context, artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a fundamental resource for refining logistical processes and increasing operational efficiency. The main purpose of the Systematic Literature Review (SLR) is to examine how the implementation of AI contributes to improving operational efficiency in the supply chains of industrial companies. To carry out this analysis, the PIOC methodology (Problem, Intervention, Outcomes, Context) was used, which facilitated the formulation of precise research questions and guided the selection of relevant studies in academic databases such as Scopus. The analysis of twenty scientific publications showed that the use of AI in demand forecasting and inventory management represents the most relevant applications, contributing 25% and 20% to overall performance, respectively. In addition, route optimization and predictive maintenance also demonstrated a considerable effect, with 15%. The results suggest that adopting AI not only increases efficiency by reducing costs and downtime, but also helps companies respond quickly to environmental changes. This study expands knowledge about the positive impact of AI on the supply chain, highlighting its importance in the current digital era. © 2025 Latin American and Caribbean Consortium of Engineering Institutions. Authors: Marino S.M.P.
Author full names: Marino, Sam M. P. (58793347000)
Title: Dynamic Warehousing: An AI Approach
Source title: Proceedings of the International Conference on Electronic Business (ICEB)
Affiliations: James Cook University, Cairns, Australia
Authors with affiliations: Marino S.M.P., James Cook University, Cairns, Australia
Abstract: This literature research paper investigates how dynamic warehousing, through artificial intelligence (AI), advances sustainable warehousing. This paper studies AI's role as a significant enabler of dynamic warehousing, concentrating on the research framework, establishing that AI drivers interacting with AI enablers advance AI-driven warehousing. It will also scrutinise AI's role in supply-side inputs and demand-side capabilities in modelling sustainable, cost-effective, smart, dynamic warehousing. With the evolution of warehousing and, in particular, the more extensive scale of facilities within the supply-demand chain, AI systems in real-time facilitate advanced warehouse operations, improving scalability, optimising storage, and enhancing fulfilment processes. This research paper examines the impact of AI on the operational efficiency of dynamic warehousing, encompassing inventory management, order fulfilment, space utilisation, and demand forecasting. The transformation of Australian warehousing to a more dynamic future that drives workflow optimisation will rely on advanced AI systems and intelligent technologies. With the growth of e-commerce and M-commerce, enhanced AI data network systems support further development of faster logistical solutions. The anticipated advantages of dynamic warehousing include agility in meeting market demands, optimised productivity and warehouse sustainability, all highlighting their importance in the future of warehousing. With the advancement of AI and associated technologies, dynamic warehousing will facilitate a shift towards greater operational effectiveness, reduced environmental impact, lowered operational costs and enhanced worker well-being © 2025, International Consortium for Electronic Business. Authors: Vaddy R.K.
Author full names: Vaddy, Rama Krishna (56403187800)
Title: AI-Powered Transformation: Revolutionizing Retail and CPG Supply Chains with Generative AI for Sustainability and Efficiency
Source title: Cutting-Edge Solutions for Advancing Sustainable Development: Exploring Technological Horizons for Sustainability - Part 1
Affiliations: Kraft Heinz Company, Chicago, 60601, IL, United States
Authors with affiliations: Vaddy R.K., Kraft Heinz Company, Chicago, 60601, IL, United States
Abstract: This book chapter delves into the transformative potential of artificial intelligence (AI) within the retail and consumer packaged goods (CPG) sectors, particularly in supply chain management. Focusing on generative AI techniques like generative adversarial networks (GANs), the chapter explores how AI-powered solutions are reshaping traditional supply chain paradigms to enhance sustainability and efficiency. By leveraging AI-driven insights, organizations can optimize processes, improve forecasting accuracy, and drive innovation across the ecosystem. Generative AI enables precise solutions to complex challenges like demand forecasting and inventory management. Furthermore, AI fosters sustainable practices through dynamic resource allocation, waste reduction, and ethical sourcing. Through case studies and real-world examples, the chapter showcases how AI innovations benefit businesses, consumers, and the environment. By collectively adopting AI technologies, organizations can propel the evolution towards smarter, more sustainable supply chains. In conclusion, the convergence of AI and supply chain management empowers retailers and CPG companies to unlock efficiencies, drive growth, and foster a sustainable future for the industry. © 2025, Bentham Books imprint.

Title: Optimizing Logistics in Supply Chains Using AI

Abstract: The global supply chain management complexity is growing, and in this regard, it warrants developing innovative solutions for logistics operations optimization. The focus of this study is the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) for logistics optimization in supply chains, through the analysis of relevant areas like route optimization, demand forecasting, inventory management, and the reduction of costs. This paper will show how advanced AI technologies, such as machine learning, predictive analytics, and deep learning, can help make better use of resources and help get the right information timely and the right decision made. AI case studies along with simulations illustrate the real benefits such as reduced transport costs, shorter delivery times, and better resource allocations. The research, in addition, deals with hurdles restricting the wide adoption of AI solutions in the area of logistics such as the integration of data, scalability issues, and ethical questions on AI use. This paper makes available a structured approach to the implementation of AI-based optimization of logistics, which supports the sustainable advancement of resilient supply chain systems.  © 2025 IEEE.
Author Keywords: Artificial intelligence; Logistics optimization; Predictive analytics; Route optimization; Supply chains

Abstract: This book discusses the transformative potential of quantum computing in reshaping the landscape of supply chain management. It bridges the gap between these two dynamic fields, offering a comprehensive guide to the application of quantum principles in supply chain operations. Through detailed examples and case studies, it highlights how quantum computing can tackle industry-specific issues, such as managing global supply chain disruptions, enhancing production schedules, and enabling real-time decision-making. This book is for researchers, professionals, and technologists interested in quantum computing and supply chain practices. Features: Provides an in-depth analysis of quantum computing technologies and their capacity to solve complex optimisation problems at scales unimaginable with traditional computing Examines the impact of quantum computing on manufacturing and logistics, with a focus on sectors such as automotive and aerospace Real-world scenarios illustrate how quantum solutions can streamline operations and drive efficiency Explores quantum algorithms and their use in addressing challenges like route optimisation, inventory management, and demand forecasting, offering strategies to reduce costs and improve resilience Considers the current limitations, ethical implications, and the path to widespread adoption of quantum computing in supply chains, emphasising the need for interdisciplinary collaboration. © 2026 selection and editorial matter, Pushan Kumar Dutta, Pronaya Bhattacharya, Jai Prakash Verma, Ashok Chopra, Neel Kanth Kundu and Khursheed Aurangzeb; individual chapters, the contributors.

Title: Research of the use of digital technologies in the logistics of the poultry subcomplex
Source title: BIO Web of Conferences
Affiliations: Ural State University of Economics, Yekaterinburg, 620144, Russian Federation
Authors with affiliations: Kondratenko I., Ural State University of Economics, Yekaterinburg, 620144, Russian Federation; Sulimin V., Ural State University of Economics, Yekaterinburg, 620144, Russian Federation; Shvedov V., Ural State University of Economics, Yekaterinburg, 620144, Russian Federation
Abstract: The article provides an in-depth analysis of how advancements in digital technology are influencing and improving the logistics operations within the poultry subcomplex. The study focuses on the use of digital technologies such as Artificial Intelligence (AI), Internet of Things (IoT), blockchain, and advanced analytics in enhancing the efficiency, transparency, and traceability of the supply chain from farm to table. The paper begins with an overview of the current state of the poultry industry and the associated logistical challenges, such as waste reduction, ensuring product quality, and managing complex supply chains. It then delves into how digital technologies can help overcome these challenges. Through the use of real-world case studies, the authors provide a comprehensive examination of the practical implementation of these technologies and their resultant impact. The study finds that AI and advanced analytics allow for better decision-making through predictive modeling and real-time data analysis, thereby improving inventory management and demand forecasting. IoT applications provide constant monitoring of storage conditions and transportation, ensuring product quality and safety. Blockchain technology enhances the transparency and traceability of the supply chain, fostering trust among consumers. © The Authors, published by EDP Sciences. Title: The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Productivity and Sustainability of SMEs: A Case Study of India
Source title: Palgrave Studies in Democracy, Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Growth
Affiliations: KLES’s Institute of Management Studies and Research, Karnataka, Hubli, India; School of Management Studies and Research, KLE Technological University, Karnataka, Hubli, India

Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has emerged as a crucial instrument for companies seeking to enhance their efficiency, creativity, and output. In recent years, there has been increased focus on the adoption of AI technologies by small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in developing countries. These SMEs often face challenges in optimizing their operations and maximizing productivity. AI can address these issues by automating repetitive tasks, providing real-time data analysis, and streamlining decision-making processes. For instance, AI-driven chatbots can efficiently handle customer inquiries, while AI-based inventory management systems can reduce costs and optimize stock levels. SMEs can leverage AI to enhance decision-making, streamline supply chain operations, and improve customer experiences. Additionally, AI-powered predictive analytics can help SMEs reduce waste by forecasting demand patterns and adjusting their inventory accordingly. This study aims to examine the impact of AI on the productivity and sustainability of SMEs in India. The research will employ descriptive design and utilize a structured questionnaire to assess the awareness and utilization of AI tools in SMEs. The results will highlight the best practices, benefits of AI implementation, and obstacles faced by SMEs when adopting AI technologies. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

Title: Integration of advanced Artificial Intelligence in Supply Chain Management, its Challenges and Opportunities

Abstract: Integrating Artificial Intelligence (AI) into Supply Chain Management (SCM) is transforming the industry by enabling smarter, faster, and more adaptable operations. Key applications include predictive analytics for demand forecasting, real-time inventory tracking, and optimization of logistics and distribution. AI-driven SCM leverages machine learning algorithms, computer vision, and natural language processing to enhance efficiency, reduce costs, and improve decision-making. AI helps manage supply chain disruptions by predicting potential risks, supporting resilience planning, and automating complex processes, creating a more robust and responsive supply chain ecosystem. This study explores the opportunities and challenges AI presents in manufacturing SCM. AI enables advanced predictive analytics, real-time inventory management, demand forecasting, and automation, leading to streamlined operations and reduced costs. However, challenges such as data privacy, high implementation costs, technological complexity, and the need for skilled labor create significant barriers to adoption. This study examines how manufacturers can leverage AI to optimize supply chain processes while addressing these challenges, highlighting best practices and strategies for successful AI adoption. Through a focused analysis, the article aims to provide insights into how AI can reshape SCM, paving the way for smarter, more adaptive manufacturing supply chains. ©20XX IEEE.

Authors: Simchi-Levi D.; Wu M.X.
Author full names: Simchi-Levi, David (7004509983); Wu, Michelle Xiao (57222721674)
Title: Powering retailers’ digitization through analytics and automation
Source title: International Journal of Production Research
Affiliations: Operations Research Center, Institute for Data, Systems and Society, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA, United States; School of Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA, United States; Carson College of Business, Washington State University, Pullman, WA, United States
Authors with affiliations: Simchi-Levi D., Operations Research Center, Institute for Data, Systems and Society, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA, United States, School of Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Cambridge, MA, United States; Wu M.X., Carson College of Business, Washington State University, Pullman, WA, United States
Abstract: Retailers face significant pressure to improve revenue, margins and market share by applying price optimisation models. These are mathematical models that calculate how demand varies at different price levels, then combine that data with information on costs and inventory levels to recommend prices that will improve revenue and profits. These models have been around for a while-so what is different now? We have identified three important changes: (1) Data: availability of internal and external real-time data such as traffic to a website, consumers making buy/no buy decisions and competitor pricing strategies; (2) Analytics: advances in machine learning and ease of access (R, Python) have enabled the development of systems that learn on the fly about consumer behaviour and preferences and generate effective estimates of demand-price relationships; and (3) Automation: increase in computing speed enables real-time optimisation of prices of hundreds of competing products sold by the same retailer. We take advantage of these new opportunities by showing how they were applied at Boston-based flash sales retailer Rue La La, online market maker Groupon, and the largest online retailer in Latin America, B2W Digital (B2W). While all these examples are of on-line businesses which have readily available data and can change prices dynamically, we have also implemented similar methods for brick-and-mortar retailed in applications such as promotional pricing, new product introduction, and assortment optimisation with similar business impacts. Thus, beyond applications to price optimisations, these new trends enable companies to revolutionise their business from procurement to supply chain all the way to revenue management. © 2017 Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

Abstract: In this vital resource with discussion of and applicability to various industries, Ali, Rabiu, and Ismail guide readers through the challenging environment of inventory management and enable them to successfully balance the demand and supply of items in stock, a critical task in any field. The book covers a wide range of topics related to inventory management and optimization. After introducing the key concepts and principles of inventory management, such as inventory analytics, optimization, and models, the authors provide a comprehensive and in-depth understanding of various inventory control techniques that are essential for effective inventory management, such as ABC analysis, EOQ model, safety stock, and reorder point. They then introduce various mathematical models and optimization techniques such as system-level and item-level inventory analysis and show how to perform sensitivity analyses to test the robustness of these models. They then look at the role of inventory management in various industries such as supply chain management and logistics, manufacturing, and more; and address the effects and integration of cutting-edge technologies like artificial intelligence, machine learning, and robotics to conventional inventory management practices. Additional topics include inventory forecasting, inventory management systems, inventory auditing and control, risk management. In combining mathematical underpinnings in the area with practical case studies throughout, readers will gain a solid understanding of the real-world applications of these different techniques so that they can apply inventory management and optimization best practices in their workplace. The comprehensive coverage makes the book a valuable reference for practitioners and students, particularly postgraduate and MBA students, who require such insights to improve business functions and make informed decisions. Because it provides the foundational mathematical knowledge required to comprehend any chapter, it is also accessible for readers without a strong background in mathematics. © 2025 Majid Khan Majahar Ali, Sani Rabiu, and Mohd Tahir Ismail.

Abstract: The results reveal that advanced machine learning models significantly outperform traditional methods, providing superior accuracy and stability in forecasts. These findings underscore AI’s potential to address challenges such as fluctuating demand and inventory management in the distribution sector. The study recommends adopting Gradient Boosting models and implementing continuous monitoring systems to optimize SCM processes. This research highlights the critical role of AI in transforming supply chains in the distribution industry and emphasizes the need for skill development to fully harness technological benefits. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2025.

Authors: Barman A.
Author full names: Barman, Abhijit (57219484639)
Title: Sustainability, Supply Chain Management, and Machine Intelligence: Overcoming Challenges and Shaping the Future
Source title: Procedia Computer Science
Affiliations: Operations and Quantitative Methods Area, Indian Institute of Management Raipur, Atal Nagar, CG, Raipur, 493661, India
Authors with affiliations: Barman A., Operations and Quantitative Methods Area, Indian Institute of Management Raipur, Atal Nagar, CG, Raipur, 493661, India
Abstract: In the modern business world, the integration of sustainable supply chain management (SSCM) with machine intelligence - encompassing AI and machine learning - marks a trans-formative and innovative convergence. This paper provides a detailed exploration of sustainable supply chain practices, addressing core principles, challenges, and the critical need for organizations to adopt environmentally responsible, ethically sourced, and socially equitable strategies. It also examines the disruptive potential of machine intelligence in optimizing supply chains, highlighting key opportunities such as improved demand forecasting, efficient inventory management, and reduced waste. The complex interplay between SSCM and machine intelligence is analyzed, emphasizing challenges like data complexities and ethical dilemmas, alongside the opportunities they present. Finally, the paper looks ahead to future trends and ethical considerations that will shape the evolution of sustainable supply chains in the era of machine intelligence. This survey aims to guide businesses, policymakers, and researchers toward developing responsible, sustainable, and effective supply chains. © 2025 The Author(s).

Authors: Delgado F.; Garrido S.; Bezerra B.S.
Author full names: Delgado, Fernanda (57822221000); Garrido, Susana (25959868500); Bezerra, Barbara Stolte (55218519100)
Title: Barriers to Visibility in Supply Chains: Challenges and Opportunities of Artificial Intelligence Driven by Industry 4.0 Technologies
Source title: Sustainability (Switzerland)
Affiliations: Production Engineering Department, São Paulo State University, Bauru, 17033-360, Brazil; Centre for Business and Economics Research (CEBER), Coimbra University, Coimbra, 3004-512, Portugal; Civil Engineering Department, São Paulo State University, Bauru, 17033-360, Brazil
Authors with affiliations: Delgado F., Production Engineering Department, São Paulo State University, Bauru, 17033-360, Brazil; Garrido S., Centre for Business and Economics Research (CEBER), Coimbra University, Coimbra, 3004-512, Portugal; Bezerra B.S., Civil Engineering Department, São Paulo State University, Bauru, 17033-360, Brazil
Abstract: Advancements in e-commerce and Industry 4.0 technologies have significantly improved communication and connectivity in supply chains. These technologies, particularly artificial intelligence driven by Industry 4.0 technologies (AI-IT4.0), have reshaped how products, services, and financial transactions are managed, emphasizing the importance of information sharing among supply chain participants to enhance inventory management, sales, and demand forecasting. However, sharing comprehensive information across supply chain stakeholders presents persistent challenges due to various barriers. This study seeks to review the current understanding of visibility barriers in supply chains, identify key obstacles, and suggest directions for future research. Using the PRISMA methodology, the study analyzed 20 articles, identifying 12 critical barriers to visibility. Bibliometric analysis has revealed growing interest in the topic since 2021, although evidence of collaborative research remains limited. A keyword co-occurrence analysis highlighted strong connections between visibility, supply chain management, sustainability, and artificial intelligence driven by industry 4.0 technologies, such as machine learning, predictive analytics, and digital twins. Future research should empirically investigate visibility barriers and explore the interplay between AI-TI4.0, visibility, and sustainable performance through case studies and quantitative approaches. © 2025 by the authors.

Authors: Dolgui A.; Ivanov D.
Author full names: Dolgui, Alexandre (57201190890); Ivanov, Dmitry (56350235000)
Title: Internet of behaviors: conceptual model, practical and theoretical implications for supply chain and operations management
Source title: International Journal of Production Research
Affiliations: Automation, Production and Computer Sciences, IMT Atlantique, LS2N - CNRS, Nantes, France; Berlin School of Economics and Law, Supply Chain and Operations Management, Berlin, Germany
Authors with affiliations: Dolgui A., Automation, Production and Computer Sciences, IMT Atlantique, LS2N - CNRS, Nantes, France; Ivanov D., Berlin School of Economics and Law, Supply Chain and Operations Management, Berlin, Germany
Abstract: The Internet of Behaviours (IoB) is a network designed and used for replicating, understanding, predicting, and influencing human behaviour through data analytics. This study aims to advance our understanding of how the IoB will impact supply chain and operations management (SCOM). Based on a combination of the existing IoB frameworks with industrial Metaverse and digital supply chain twins, we propose a framework for IoB in SCOM encompassing management, organisation, and technology dimensions. We conclude that the IoB will impact a multitude of SCOM areas such as supply chain design, demand forecasting, sales and operations planning, distribution and transportation, inventory and production management, sourcing strategy and purchasing, vehicle routeing, supply chain resilience and sustainability. We offer a structured future research agenda pointing to new research questions and topics stemming from IoB-driven real-time visibility, data analytics, and remote control. Methodology of SCOM can also be enriched by the IoB through a combination of artificial intelligence and digital technology with operations research and behavioural operations management. © 2024 Informa UK Limited, trading as Taylor & Francis Group.

Authors: Fatorachian H.; Shokri A.
Author full names: Fatorachian, Hajar (57200375677); Shokri, Alireza (55979295100)
Title: Waste generation patterns and mitigation strategies in cold chains
Source title: Journal of Environmental Management
Affiliations: Leeds Beckett University, United Kingdom; Northumbria University, United Kingdom
Authors with affiliations: Fatorachian H., Leeds Beckett University, United Kingdom; Shokri A., Northumbria University, United Kingdom
Abstract: This study explores waste generation patterns in cold chain logistics, emphasizing the interrelationships between product categories, promotional activities, and inventory inefficiencies. Using real-world data from Company A, a comprehensive methodological approach, including time-series analysis and Ordinary Least Squares (OLS) regression, was employed to identify critical drivers of waste. The findings demonstrate that promotional activities significantly increase waste levels, notably through overproduction and misaligned demand forecasting. Furthermore, clear seasonal patterns emerged, pinpointing specific periods of peak waste linked to promotions and festive demand spikes. The analysis also highlighted warehouse inefficiencies as key contributors to waste, suggesting targeted logistical optimizations as essential. The study's novelty lies in its application of the Technology-Organization-Environment (TOE) framework to structure insights into AI-driven waste reduction strategies specifically tailored for cold chain operations. Unlike existing research, this study integrates AI-powered predictive analytics, sustainable packaging solutions, and waste categorization models, offering an empirically validated, actionable framework for supply chain managers. These results contribute significantly to existing literature by moving beyond generic operational improvements, directly addressing how technological, organizational, and regulatory factors collectively influence waste mitigation. The practical implications highlight the necessity for organizations to adopt a holistic, technology-enabled, and sustainability-oriented approach, ensuring long-term economic and environmental benefits. © 2025 The Author(s)

Authors: Gerald Manju J.; Chitra P.; Selva Jeba G.
Title: A Hybrid Generative AI Demand Forecasting Framework for Mitigating the Bullwhip Effect in Supply Chain Operations
Source title: Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems
Affiliations: Thiagarajar College of Engineering, Tamil Nadu, Madurai, India
Authors with affiliations: Gerald Manju J., Thiagarajar College of Engineering, Tamil Nadu, Madurai, India; Chitra P., Thiagarajar College of Engineering, Tamil Nadu, Madurai, India; Selva Jeba G., Thiagarajar College of Engineering, Tamil Nadu, Madurai, India
Abstract: As global markets become increasingly complex and fast-paced, effective Supply Chain Management (SCM) is essential for synchronizing production, distribution and customer fulfilment. A reliable demand forecasting is essential to SCM in order to effectively utilize inventories and minimize operational inefficiencies. The bullwhip effect, which occurs when minor fluctuations in demand are magnified upstream due to poor forecasting, results in excess inventory and stockouts. Dynamic market patterns cannot be handled by conventional techniques. In this work, a Hybrid Generative AI Framework is presented, utilizing Bi-directional Recurrent Neural Networks (BRNNs) for demand forecasting and Variational Autoencoders (VAE) for latent feature extraction. Through the use of deep learning and generative AI, with synthetic data augmentation, the model stabilizes supply chains, achieving a Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 1.5486, Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of 2.4737 and an R-squared (R2) value of 0.9996 thereby offering an adaptable way to lessen the bullwhip impact for effective inventory management. This methodology captures time-dependent demand patterns and adapts to changing market dynamics, allowing for informed and strategic decisions. The presented framework exhibits strong generalization capabilities by modelling complex, non-linear relationships and simulating plausible demand fluctuations. Ultimately, it contributes to minimization of surplus stock, lowering of energy consumption from excess production and improving product availability for end consumers. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

Abstract: In the face of increasingly complex and volatile supply chains, artificial intelligence has emerged as a strategic lever for businesses. By analyzing vast amounts of real-time data, AI enables process optimization, improved demand forecasting accuracy, and disruption anticipation. From production to logistics and inventory management, AI is profoundly transforming various segments of the supply chain. Companies adopting these technologies gain agility, reduce costs, and enhance customer satisfaction. This study delves into the specific segments of the supply chain impacted by AI between 2018 and 2024, as well as the prevalent techniques employed during this period. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

Authors: Azari M.; Arif J.; Moustabchir H.; Jawab F.
Author full names: Azari, Meriem (57988075700); Arif, Jabir (44860956700); Moustabchir, Hassane (23019496400); Jawab, Fouad (44861244000)
Title: Navigating Challenges and Leveraging Future Trends in AI and Machine Learning for Supply Chains
Source title: AI and Machine Learning Applications in Supply Chains and Marketing
Affiliations: Superior School of Technology, Morocco; National School of Applied Science, Morocco
Authors with affiliations: Azari M., Superior School of Technology, Morocco; Arif J., Superior School of Technology, Morocco; Moustabchir H., National School of Applied Science, Morocco; Jawab F., Superior School of Technology, Morocco
Abstract: This chapter explores the transformative impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) on supply chain management, highlighting their ability to optimize demand forecasting, inventory management, supplier relationships, and logistics operations. These technologies drive efficiency, cost savings, and enhanced customer satisfaction through accurate predictions and automated decision- making. However, the chapter also addresses significant implementation challenges, including high costs, data quality issues, technological complexity, and ethical concerns such as data privacy and algorithmic bias. Through case studies of companies like Amazon, DHL, Walmart, Siemens, and Starbucks, the chapter illustrates practical strategies for overcoming these obstacles. It concludes with recommendations for successful AI and ML integration, emphasizing the need for robust data governance, scalable solutions, and a culture of innovation to fully harness these technologies for long- term success and sustainability in supply chain management. © 2025 by IGI Global Scientific Publishing. All rights reserved.

Title: Catalyzing Supply Chain Evolution: A Comprehensive Examination of Artificial Intelligence Integration in Supply Chain Management †
Source title: Engineering Proceedings
Affiliations: Department of Computer Science, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States; Department of Administration Sciences, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States
Authors with affiliations: Pattnaik S., Department of Computer Science, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States; Liew N., Department of Computer Science, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States; Kures A.O., Department of Computer Science, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States; Pinsky E., Department of Computer Science, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States; Park K., Department of Administration Sciences, Metropolitan College, Boston University, Boston, 02215, MA, United States
Abstract: The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into Supply-Chain Management (SCM) has revolutionized operations, offering avenues for enhanced efficiency and decision-making. AI has become pivotal in tackling various Supply-Chain Management challenges, notably enhancing demand forecasting precision and automating warehouse operations for improved efficiency and error reduction. However, a critical debate arises concerning the choice between less accurate explainable models and more accurate yet unexplainable models in Supply-Chain Management applications. This paper explores this debate within the context of various Supply-Chain Management challenges and proposes a methodology for developing models tailored to different Supply-Chain Management problems. Drawing from academic research and modelling, the paper discusses the applications of AI in demand forecasting, inventory optimization, warehouse automation, transportation management, supply chain planning, supplier management, quality control, risk management, and customer service. Additionally, it examines the trade-offs between model interpretability and accuracy, highlighting the need for a nuanced approach. The proposed methodology advocates for the development of explainable models for tasks where interpretability is crucial, such as risk management and supplier selection, while leveraging unexplainable models for tasks prioritizing accuracy, like demand forecasting and predictive maintenance. Through this approach, stakeholders gain insights into Supply-Chain Management processes, fostering better decision-making and accountability. © 2024 by the authors.

Authors: Maharana S.
Author full names: Maharana, Sudeept (59906782100)
Title: Consumer behavior and demand shocks: Role of panic buying, stockpiling, and shifts in consumer preferences
Source title: Supply Chain Disruptions and Impact on Global Inflation
Affiliations: Indian Institute of Technology, India
Authors with affiliations: Maharana S., Indian Institute of Technology, India
Abstract: Consumer behavior is increasingly shaped by external shocks like economic crises, pandemics, and supply chain disruptions, leading to panic buying, stockpiling, and demand fluctuations. This chapter examines how Artificial Intelligence (AI) analyzes, predicts, and manages these shifts to enhance market stability and organizational resilience. AI-driven predictive analytics and sentiment analysis help businesses anticipate panic buying and stockpiling, enabling proactive inventory and supply chain management. AI-powered recommendation systems and real-time market analysis track shifting consumer preferences, optimizing pricing and engagement strategies. By integrating AI into demand forecasting, organizations improve supply chain resilience, reduce market volatility, and enhance customer retention. As AI evolves, its role in mitigating demand shocks and behavioral shifts becomes critical for economic and business sustainability, fostering adaptive, customer-centric markets. © 2025, IGI Global Scientific Publishing.

Authors: Poo M.C.-P.; Qi B.
Author full names: Poo, Mark Ching-Pong (57197705236); Qi, Baomin (57218923761)
Title: Review on the Applications of AI in Laboratory Experiments in Supply Chain Management Education
Source title: 2023 4th International Conference on Computers and Artificial Intelligence Technology, CAIT 2023
Affiliations: Liverpool Hope University, Liverpool Hope Business School, Liverpool, United Kingdom
Authors with affiliations: Poo M.C.-P., Liverpool Hope University, Liverpool Hope Business School, Liverpool, United Kingdom; Qi B., Liverpool Hope University, Liverpool Hope Business School, Liverpool, United Kingdom
Abstract: This paper comprehensively reviews the profound impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on laboratory sessions within supply chain management education. The primary objective is to elucidate the diverse applications of AI in educational settings and assess how AI has reshaped traditional laboratory sessions. This paper uses a systematic review methodology to analyse literature sources, identifying key trends, methodologies, and outcomes. The analysis categorises and scrutinises supply chain education laboratory sessions, including inventory management, demand forecasting, logistics optimisation, and procurement strategies. The paper delves into how AI technologies, such as machine learning algorithms, optimisation models, and simulation tools, have revolutionised these laboratory sessions. Findings reveal that AI integration has substantially improved the quality and effectiveness of laboratory experiments. AI-powered sessions empower students with enhanced problem-solving skills and a deeper understanding of real-world supply chain challenges. Moreover, AI-driven experiments create dynamic and adaptive learning environments, fostering student engagement and critical thinking. The implications extend beyond the classroom, as AI in laboratory sessions enhances supply chain management education and prepares future professionals for an AI-driven industry. This paper underscores the need for continued research and innovation in AI applications for supply chain management education and emphasises the importance of educators staying updated with evolving AI technologies.  © 2023 IEEE.

Title: Artificial intelligence optimizes supply chains in search of predictive and sustainable logistics
Source title: Proceedings of 2025 International Symposium on Artificial Intelligence and Computational Social Sciences, AICSS 2025
Affiliations: Faculty of Business Engineering, Universidad Continental, Huancayo, Peru
Authors with affiliations: Hidalgo Panez K.M., Faculty of Business Engineering, Universidad Continental, Huancayo, Peru; Cuadrado Ramirez D.B., Faculty of Business Engineering, Universidad Continental, Huancayo, Peru; Tocas Valero J.C., Faculty of Business Engineering, Universidad Continental, Huancayo, Peru; Ruiz Parejas R.A., Faculty of Business Engineering, Universidad Continental, Huancayo, Peru
Abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) currently represents an essential tool for optimizing supply chains, enhancing the accuracy of demand forecasting, improving inventory management efficiency, and significantly reducing operational costs. In this context, the aim of this study is to conduct a systematic literature review on the impact of AI in logistics, with particular emphasis on sustainability and predictive efficiency. For this purpose, 20 academic articles published in indexed journals between January 2023 and March 2025 were analyzed, utilizing keywords such as logistics management, supply chain optimization, predictive logistics, sustainable logistics, and artificial intelligence. The results indicate that AI implementation considerably strengthens supply chain resilience, effectively reduces the carbon footprint, and significantly optimizes resource allocation and distribution. These findings emphasize the relevance of AI as a critical component for achieving predictive and sustainable logistics in supply chains.  © 2025 Copyright held by the owner/author(s).

Title: A Systematic Literature Review on Artificial Intelligence Applications for Pharmaceutical Supply Chain Performance
Source title: The Role of AI in Sustainable Supply Chain Management
Affiliations: National School of Applied Sciences, Berrechid, Morocco; Moroccan School of Engineering, Morocco; Sidi Mohamed Ben Abdellah University, Morocco

Abstract: Pharmaceutical supply chains face regulatory, logistical, and transparency challenges. AI integration offers transformative solutions, enhancing demand forecasting, inventory management, and logistics efficiency. While AI improves operational resilience and risk mitigation, adoption barriers—data privacy, regulatory complexity, and costs—persist. Collaboration among stakeholders (pharma firms, tech providers, policymakers) is vital to address technical, ethical, and compliance gaps. Prioritizing scalable AI tools and partnerships can overcome these hurdles, fostering agile, transparent supply networks. Strategic AI adoption strengthens global medication access, healthcare outcomes, and supply chain sustainability. This study highlights AI’s urgency in building resilient pharma operations amid evolving market demands and patient needs. © 2026 by IGI Global Scientific Publishing. All rights reserved.

Title: Towards Adaptive Inventory Systems - A Hybrid Machine Learning Framework for Seasonal Demand and Order Optimization
Source title: International Conference on Computer Science and Engineering, UBMK
Affiliations: Ar-Ge, VBT Yazilim A.Ş., İstanbul, Turkey
Authors with affiliations: Cayli O., Ar-Ge, VBT Yazilim A.Ş., İstanbul, Turkey
Abstract: This study introduces an integrated approach aimed at addressing the challenges faced by small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) in the retail sector with respect to inventory management processes, through the application of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) techniques. In this study, four distinct ML algorithms have been combined within a hybrid framework and validated using real-world sales data. The findings demonstrate the robust performance of the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) network for seasonal demand forecasting, the balanced speed and accuracy of the Gradient Boosting Machines (GBM), the classification efficiency of Support Vector Machines (SVM), and the adaptive optimization capability of Reinforcement Learning (RL) in dynamic environments. The results indicate that the proposed model offers a viable solution for SMEs to reduce inventory-related costs and enhance supply chain efficiency.  © 2025 IEEE.

Author full names: Lal, Sudhanand Prasad (57190620499); Borah, Abhinab (59405524000); Pundir, Sakshi (58162632600); Das, Leezamoni (59405893700); Srivastava, Diksha (59262479400)
Title: Origin and history of AI, IoT and blockchain technology and their pertinence in food supply chain management
Source title: The Future of Agriculture: IoT, AI and Blockchain Technology for Sustainable Farming
Affiliations: Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University, Pusa, Bihar, India
Authors with affiliations: Lal S.P., Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University, Pusa, Bihar, India; Borah A., Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University, Pusa, Bihar, India; Pundir S., Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University, Pusa, Bihar, India; Das L., Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University, Pusa, Bihar, India; Srivastava D., Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University, Pusa, Bihar, India
Abstract: In a world where hunger and malnourishment are still a matter of concern, food loss, wastage, and its timely availability must be addressed immediately. Based on an extensive literature review and secondary data, this manuscript has tried to come up with holistic, contemporary solutions for food supply chain management. Against this backdrop, tools viz., IoT, AI, and blockchain will not only manage food loss, wastage, and timely availability but also revamp transparency, food quality and safety. IoT architecture offers tracking solutions through measurement, network, service, and application layers using various sensors and WiFi-connected devices. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and the Internet of Things are becoming significant facilitators of supply chain management (SCM) optimization. The adoption of AI technology has been associated with a 15% reduction in logistics costs, a 35% reduction in inventory levels, and a 65% increase in customer service levels. It assists in demand forecasting, enhanced safety, cost saving, boosting revenue, and chiefly on-time delivery. Block Chain Technology (BCT) system can be programmed to record and track food supply chain transactions. Chhattisgarh in India has developed the Centralized Online Real¬Time Electronic Public Distribution System (CORE-PDS) and has become a model state in the 'Public Distribution System (PDS)'. Unfortunately, Chhattisgarh's model fell prey to scams viz., bogus ration cards, irregularities in rice stock, and poor quality rice and salt samples collected, which were unsuitable for human consumption because of no mid-term audit and monitoring. The chapter concludes that all these anomalies would not have happened to such an extent or have been addressed timely if the technological trinity viz., IoT, AI, and Blockchain, had been incorporated judiciously into the PDS system. © 2024 Bentham Science Publishers. Abstract: EcoChain is a top-to-bottom blockchain-powered supply chain management system relying on IoT sensors, edge computing, AI-enabled demand forecasting, smart contracts, and blockchain technology for solving modern-day supply chain issues. It tries to enhance visibility, support instantaneous decision-making, optimize use of resources, and automate requisite procedures. Blockchain provides a secure tamper-evident ledger of decentralized information that enables transactions and IoT sensors to monitor continuous monitoring of conditions on products including location and temperature. Edge computing processes the data in real-time locally to reduce latency and enables real-time anomaly detection. The system utilizes AI models such as ARIMA for accurate demand forecasting and synchronizes production with real-time demand to prevent wastage. It automates critical processes such as payment, restocking inventory, and shipment approvals through smart contracts, reducing human intervention. In a simulated environment, EcoChain showed robust demand forecasting accuracy (96–98%) and minimal decision latency (2.5–4 s per transaction) as against traditional supply chain systems. The end-to-end technology integration in EcoChain is more scalable, open, and efficient in operation. © The Author(s), under exclusive license to Springer Nature Switzerland AG 2026.

Abstract: This chapter explores the transformative potential of integrating artificial intelligence (AI) with quantum computing to revolutionize inventory management practices. In today's dynamic business environment, efficient inventory management is paramount for organizations seeking to meet customer demand while minimizing costs and maximizing profitability. By harnessing AI's cognitive capabilities and quantum computing's computational prowess, this chapter elucidates how organizations can achieve unprecedented levels of optimization in their inventory operations. From demand forecasting and order fulfillment to supply chain optimization and risk mitigation, AI-driven quantum computing empowers organizations to make data-driven decisions with unparalleled accuracy and agility. Through real-world examples and case studies, this chapter showcases the tangible benefits of leveraging AI and quantum computing in inventory management, paving the way for enhanced operational efficiency, improved customer satisfaction, and sustainable competitive advantage in the marketplace. © 2024 by IGI Global. Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is transforming logistics and supply chain management (SCM) by enhancing efficiency, automation, and decision-making. AI-driven technologies such as machine learning, deep learning, reinforcement learning, and robotics are optimizing key areas, including demand forecasting, inventory management, transportation planning, supplier risk assessment, and last-mile delivery. Emerging innovations like AI-powered blockchain integration and quantum computing further enhance transparency, security, and computational efficiency in supply chain networks. Despite these advancements, challenges such as data integration, cybersecurity risks, ethical concerns, and high implementation costs remain. This chapter explores AI's role in logistics, its benefits, challenges, and future trends. By leveraging AI-driven solutions, businesses can achieve smarter, more resilient, and sustainable supply chains in an increasingly digitalized world. © 2026 by IGI Global Scientific Publishing. Title: The Sustainable Supply Chain: AI for Transparency and Efficiency in Eco-Conscious Business Practices

Abstract: A sustainable supply chain is one of the most rapidly growing factors in business practice. A sustainable supply chain refers to the integration of environmental practices on social, economic, financial considerations into the sourcing, production, and distribution of goods and services. The chapter explores the transformative power of artificial intelligence (AI) in revolutionizing sustainable supply chain management (SSCM). As environmental and social responsibility gain precedence, businesses face the challenge of building transparent and efficient operations. The chapter examines how AI applications can empower eco-conscious business practices and investigates how this technology fosters transparency across the supply chain. Real-time tracking, data analysis, and machine learning algorithms can illuminate potential environmental and social risks at every stage. This empowers businesses to source ethically, minimize waste, and optimize resource utilization. Furthermore, the chapter explores how AI enhances efficiency in SSCM. Predictive analytics can streamline demand forecasting, leading to optimal inventory management and reduced transportation emissions. In addition, bibliometric analysis using VoS viewer data analysis can illustrate how sustainable supply chains influence AI technology. AI-powered automation can optimize logistics and production processes, minimizing waste and energy consumption. The chapter acknowledges the challenges of AI adoption, including data privacy concerns, integration costs, and the need for skilled personnel. However, it proposes solutions to these and highlights the immense potential of AI to create a win-win scenario for businesses and the environment. The chapter is a valuable resource for business leaders, policymakers, and researchers seeking to leverage AI for a more sustainable future. It provides insights and practical applications to navigate the exciting and evolving landscape of AI-powered sustainable supply chains. © 2026 selection and editorial matter, Sonal Trivedi, Balamurugan Balusamy, Krishnaraj Nagappan, Dinesh Krishnan Subramaniam and Daniel Arockiam; individual chapters, the contributors. Abstract: Sustainable supply chain management (SSCM) has evolved from a niche concern into a strategic imperative as firms confront intensifying environmental regulations, heightened social expectations, and the need for resilient, cost-efficient operations. This chapter offers a synthesis of how artificial intelligence (AI) is reshaping SSCM by transforming data-rich yet opaque supply networks into transparent, self-optimizing ecosystems. After grounding the discussion in triple bottom line theory and established SSCM frameworks, we survey state-of-the-art AI techniques including machine learning for demand and risk forecasting, deep learning-driven computer vision for quality assurance, reinforcement learning for dynamic inventory and routing, natural language processing for supplier due-diligence automation, and robotics for low-waste, energy-aware warehouse operations. Building on this technical overview, we introduce an integration architecture that fuses AI with Internet-of-Things sensing, blockchain-secured traceability, and Industry 4.0 digital-twin platforms, enabling real-time, end-to-end sustainability optimization. Comparative case analyses across manufacturing, logistics, and retail illustrate up to double-digit reductions in carbon emissions, material waste, and lead-time variability when AI is embedded within supply chain decision loops. The chapter also provides a critical appraisal of unresolved challenges data governance, algorithmic bias, worker-centric ethics, integration costs, and escalating cybersecurity threats and proposes a responsible-AI framework that aligns technical metrics with societal goals. We conclude by mapping future research frontiers: multi-objective generative design of supply chain networks, AI mechanisms for quantifying social sustainability impacts, and human AI collaboration models that preserve managerial judgment while leveraging computational scale. Collectively, the analysis demonstrates that, when deployed within robust ethical and governance guardrails, AI can move SSCM beyond incremental efficiency gains toward transformative, regenerative supply networks capable of meeting 21st-century sustainability ambitions. © 2026 selection and editorial matter, El Arbi Abdellaoui Alaoui, Cédric Stéphane Tekouabou  Koumetio, Abdelaaziz Hessane and Amine Sallah; individual chapters, the contributors.

Title: Collaborative Forecasting with Reinforcement Learning to Enhance Resilience

Abstract: As the worldwide business environment is getting more complex, supply chains need to enhance resilient operations to face new troublesome, disruptive events, such as significant changes in sourcing, inventory management, and production planning. Supply Chain Collaboration has been an important strategic action to achieve resilience; however, it still has many challenges. One major issue is forecasting collaboration, where reaching common goals has proven difficult. Several companies have experienced diverse forces and directions depending on individual collaborators' goals, leading to weak forecasting processes that hinder the alignment of all the functional plans throughout the supply chain. In this research, we propose a reinforcement learning model to help demand planners select the best collaboration inputs from other stakeholders to improve forecasting accuracy, help maintain a balance between supply and demand and increase resilience in the planning process. The main objective of this article is to exemplify how companies can use artificial intelligence to improve collaborative forecasting and foster enterprise and supply chain resilience. Moreover, an essential contribution of the present research is the enhancement of collaborative forecasting through reinforcement learning and deploying its combined techniques in an accessible web tool for practitioners and academics. Further research will involve the complete implementation of the proposed collaborative tool in, at least, one company already engaged in internal sales and operations planning or collaborative planning and forecasting replenishment. © 2025 IEEE.

Title: Integrative Framework to Enhance Supply Chain Resilience through Advanced Forecasting Anomaly Detection and Optimized Resource Allocation

Abstract: Global supply chains face increasing complexity and vulnerability to disruptions, necessitating more robust management approaches. This study aims to evaluate the effectiveness of artificial intelligence (AI) technologies in enhancing supply chain resilience through improved prediction capabilities and automated response mechanisms. The research investigates the application of AI-based methods across three critical dimensions: predictive accuracy, disruption detection, and dynamic resource allocation. The proposed framework achieved a mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of 4.5% in demand forecasting, supporting more stable inventory management and reducing the likelihood of stockouts and overstock. Anomaly detection was performed with an 88% sensitivity and a 7% false-positive rate, allowing early intervention in potential disruptions, which resulted in a 12% reduction in downtime and 9% cost savings in affected nodes. Furthermore, the dynamic resource allocation model yielded a 16% reduction in disruption-related costs and a 17-21% decrease in lead times during demand surges. These findings suggest that integrating AI in supply chain management provides a robust, adaptive approach to operational stability, equipping supply chains to better navigate demand volatility and unforeseen disruptions  © 2025 Copyright held by the owner/author(s).

Abstract: The challenges that are involved in the China-Kyrgyzstan Medical E-commerce Innovation Project include the allocation of resources in an uneven manner, the lack of data standards in a multi-tier medical management, which prevent effectively collaborating and conducting e-commerce operations in the pharmaceutical supply chain. In this paper, a three-stage management model is developed using theory of medical management collaboration and digital governance model: data-driven-process integration-intelligent monitoring. Standard drug procurement and inventory management is done through the Enterprise Resource Planning (ERP) system. Second, cross-departmental and cross-institutional business processes are integrated on a Business Process Management (BPM) platform to provide a real-time data exchange between various parties. Lastly, there is the introduction of Artificial Intelligence (AI) algorithms which are used to optimize pharmaceutical demand forecasting and schedule logistics. The project was tested at the same time in Jiangsu and Bishkek, Kyrgyzstan. After the system went live, overall operational efficiency improved by 30.1%, drug procurement cycle shortened by 42.6%, inventory capital occupancy rate decreased by 8.8 percentage points, and information flow time was shortened by 41.8%. This model effectively improves the collaboration and responsiveness of the medical e-commerce system, providing replicable experience and pathways for the management and implementation of innovative medical e-commerce projects. © 2026, Success Culture Press. Author full names: Ponte, Borja (56029133100); Ruano, Laura (56644574200); Pino, Raúl (7006690508); De Fuentela, David (56644995400)
Title: The Bullwhip effect in water demand management: Taming it through an artificial neural networks-based system
Source title: Journal of Water Supply: Research and Technology - AQUA
Affiliations: Polytechnic School of Engineering, University of Oviedo, Campus de Viesques S/n, Gijón, 33204, Spain
Authors with affiliations: Ponte B., Polytechnic School of Engineering, University of Oviedo, Campus de Viesques S/n, Gijón, 33204, Spain; Ruano L., Polytechnic School of Engineering, University of Oviedo, Campus de Viesques S/n, Gijón, 33204, Spain; Pino R., Polytechnic School of Engineering, University of Oviedo, Campus de Viesques S/n, Gijón, 33204, Spain; De Fuentela D., Polytechnic School of Engineering, University of Oviedo, Campus de Viesques S/n, Gijón, 33204, Spain
Abstract: The Bullwhip effect (BE) refers to the amplification of the variance of orders and inventories along the supply chain as they move away from the customer. This is considered as the main cause of inefficiencies in the management of a traditional supply chain. However, the BE is not relevant in the classic system of water distribution, based on long-term supply management. Nevertheless, current circumstances have drawn a new context, which has introduced the concept of water demand management, in which efficiency and sustainability are of great importance. Then, the time horizon of management has decreased enormously and the supply time takes on an important role. Therefore, the BE must be considered, as it significantly raises the costs of management. On the one hand, this paper brings evidence that the BE appears in a system of real-time management of water demand. On the other hand, it proposes the application of artificial intelligence techniques for its reduction. More specifically, an advanced forecasting system based on artificial neural networks has been used. The BE is heavily damped. © IWA Publishing 2015.

Title: Digital Transformation in Supply Chain: Contemporary Applications, Strategic Contributions, and Emerging Challenges
Source title: Digital Transformation of Business World: A Managerial Perspective
Affiliations: Amity University, Noida, India; Jaypee Business School, Noida, India
Authors with affiliations: Shivanjali, Amity University, Noida, India; Singh M., Jaypee Business School, Noida, India
Abstract: Digital transformation is reshaping the supply chain landscape, driving enhanced efficiency, visibility, and responsiveness. This study explores contemporary applications of digital technologies such as the Internet of Things (loT), artificial intelligence (AI), blockchain, and cloud computing in supply chain operations. These technologies enable real-time tracking, predictive analytics, and secure transactions, fostering greater collaboration and transparency across the value chain. Strategic contributions of digital transformation include the optimisation of logistics, demand forecasting, inventory management, and customer service, which collectively enhance competitiveness and adaptability in volatile markets. However, emerging challenges such as cybersecurity risks, data privacy concerns, technology integration complexities, and workforce upskilling demand critical attention. This chapter provides insights into balancing technological innovation with strategic resilience to unlock the full potential of digital transformation in the supply chain. © 2025 by Shivanjalia and Mitushi Singhb. Abstract: The abstracts and articles found provide insights into the use of intelligent inventory management and demand forecasting. The research emphasises the importance of demand forecasting in preventing issues such as overstocking or understocking. It discusses the use of artificial intelligence, machine learning models, and forecasting techniques to accurately predict future demand, reduce costs, optimise inventory, and minimise environmental impact. The studies highlight the value of integrating AI models into supply chain management to improve overall operational efficiency and sustainability. The research presented in the articles demonstrates the potential of intelligent inventory management and demand forecasting to significantly impact organisations' bottom line by improving efficiency, reducing costs, and minimising environmental impact. © 2025 Author(s).

Author full names: Aktas, Emel (14059584200); Chomachaei, Fahimeh (58500300100); Golmohammadi, Davood (24528216400)
Title: Life Cycle Prediction: A Comparison of Methods for a Lighting Products Retailer
Source title: IEEE Transactions on Engineering Management
Affiliations: Cranefield University, Cranfield, MK43 0AL, United Kingdom; College of Management, University of Massachusetts Boston, Boston, 02125, MA, United States
Authors with affiliations: Aktas E., Cranefield University, Cranfield, MK43 0AL, United Kingdom; Chomachaei F., College of Management, University of Massachusetts Boston, Boston, 02125, MA, United States; Golmohammadi D., College of Management, University of Massachusetts Boston, Boston, 02125, MA, United States
Abstract: Product life cycle (PLC) prediction is one of the most challenging yet critically important aspects of supply chain management. Lost sales and excess inventory costs arise when there is a mismatch between demand and supply, especially at the beginning of a product’s life cycle when a new product is launched. Our proposed framework contributes to the application of decision-support systems in the prediction of PLCs of new products. In this study, we fit piecewise-linear curves, nth order polynomial curves, and Bass diffusion curves for PLC prediction and compare their effectiveness using real data from a retailer specializing in lighting products. We estimate the PLCs of 2 615 lighting products using these models and select the best-fit curve to predict their PLCs. We also develop an algorithm to address challenges posed by imbalanced datasets and apply it in neural networks for predictive modeling to determine a product’s PLC stage, demand class, and stocking decisions. The findings show that fourth-order polynomial curves can accurately predict the PLCs of 63% of the products. Bass diffusion curves emerge as the second-best performer. Our approach can be generalized to other products in other industries, and it can effectively guide end-of-life inventory decisions. © 2025 IEEE. All rights reserved.

Title: Applications and Prospects of Artificial Intelligence in the Food Industry; [人工智能在食品产业中的应用及展望]

Abstract: With the rapid development of artificial intelligence (AI) technology, its application in the food industry has become increasingly widespread, covering various segments from breeding and cultivation, production and processing, safety testing and regulation, to supply chain management and consumer experience. This paper reviewed the key technological research and application progress of AI in the food industry chain, and discussed its future development directions and challenges in the food industry. AI technology has improved the efficiency and quality of agricultural production through precision agriculture, intelligent agricultural equipment, smart breeding, and yield prediction. At the same time, in the field of food production and processing, AI has enhanced production efficiency and product safety through automated production lines, production data analysis, and forecasting. In the field of food safety regulation, AI, combined with sensor technology, traceability systems, and anomaly detection technology, has enhanced the traceability and safety of food. In terms of supply chain management, the application of AI in demand forecasting, inventory management, and logistics planning has improved the response speed and stability of the supply chain. Additionally, AI applications in consumer preference analysis and personalized recommendations have improved the shopping experience and provided valuable consumer insights for businesses. Although AI technology has shown great potential and advantages in the food industry, it also faces risks and challenges such as data privacy and security, technology popularity, and regulatory standards, which require the food industry, government departments, and the academic community to jointly formulate corresponding policies and standards. In the future, AI technology will continue to be a key driving force for innovation and sustainable development in the food industry, promoting the construction of a healthier, safer, and more efficient food ecosystem. © 2025, Chinese Institute of Food Science and Technology. Authors: Sharma T.; Ojha M.K.
Author full names: Sharma, Tanmay (59398140900); Ojha, Manish Kumar (57208641590)
Title: Integration of IoT & Blockchain Using AI for Optimizing Multi-Echelon Inventory System
Source title: 2025 3rd International Conference on Communication, Security, and Artificial Intelligence, ICCSAI 2025
Affiliations: Amity University, Department of Ai, Aset, Noida, India
Authors with affiliations: Sharma T., Amity University, Department of Ai, Aset, Noida, India; Ojha M.K., Amity University, Department of Ai, Aset, Noida, India
Abstract: Efficient inventory control in multi-tier systems is critical to increasing operational performance and lowering costs. The introduction of new technologies such as IoT, Blockchain, and AI is a viable strategy to deal with the complexities of these systems. This study presents a unified system that uses blockchain technology for safe data exchange and automated processes, IoT for real-time inventory tracking, and artificial intelligence (AI) -more especially the reinforcement learning algorithm Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) - for cost optimization and demand forecasting. By integrating these technologies, the study suggests a system that reduces holding and ordering expenses, enhances decision making, and increases visibility across various levels of the supply chain. Simulation results show significant improvements in cost-effectiveness and inventory management.In addition to cutting expenses and improving operational efficiency, this article tackles sustainability by decreasing waste, lowering the carbon footprint through accurate demand forecasting, and improving resource efficiency in multi-tier systems. This model highlights the potential of Industry 4.0 technologies to transform traditional supply chain systems.  © 2025 IEEE.

Ramakrishnan College of Engineering, Department of Ai & Ds, Tamil Nadu, Trichy, India
Abstract: In today's complex global economy, supply chain management (SCM) efficiency, transparency and security face enormous challenges. The benefits of emerging technologies such as reinforcement learning (RL) and blockchain provide a transformational approach to these issues. This study explores the integration of RL and blockchain technologies to enhance the SCM process. Reinforcement learning, a subset of machine learning, enables systems to learn optimal design through trial and error interactions with dynamic environments When applied to SCM, RL algorithms can continuously learn from supply chain data and adjust processes in real-time for routing, optimal inventory management, and demand forecasting. Blockchain technology supports RL by creating a decentralized and immutable ledger that provides transparency and security throughout the supply chain. Every transaction from raw materials to end products is recorded in the blockchain, creating an accountable process that increases trust among stakeholders, decreases it fraud and integrates smart contracts to keep the transaction automated, ensure compliance with predetermined conditions and increase operational efficiency. This paper presents a framework for integrating RL and blockchain in SCM, focusing on the benefits of integrating enterprises with these technologies. The proposed approach demonstrates how RL can enable adaptive intelligent decision making, while ensuring the integrity and transparency of blockchain data.  © 2024 IEEE.
